title: Dissociation and Trauma: What It Feels Like and How to Come Back
excerpt: Explore the experience of dissociation in trauma and learn practical, somatic-based ways to regain connection with your body and mind.
body:
Understanding Dissociation: The Body's Way of Coping

Dissociation is a complex, often misunderstood response to trauma. It acts as a psychological and physiological defense mechanism, allowing a person to detach from the overwhelming reality of their experience. As Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, explains, dissociation is the mind’s way of protecting itself when the body and brain are flooded with unbearable stress.

When trauma overwhelms the nervous system, dissociation can feel like a fog, a numbness, or a sense of being disconnected from your own body or reality. For some, it shows up as memory gaps; for others, it’s a feeling of watching your life from the outside, as if you’re not really “there.”

What Dissociation Feels Like: Voices from the Body

People who experience dissociation often describe it in terms like:

  • Feeling like a ghost inside your own body
  • Time slipping away or moments feeling unreal
  • Emotional numbness or a sense of being ‘shut down’
  • Out-of-body experiences or looking at yourself from a distance
  • Difficulty recalling what happened during traumatic events

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, points out that dissociation is not a sign of mental illness but an adaptive survival strategy. It’s the nervous system’s way of freezing or escaping when fight or flight isn’t possible.

The Neuroscience Behind Dissociation

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory offers crucial insight into how dissociation arises from the nervous system’s hierarchical response to threat. When both fight and flight responses are unavailable or ineffective, the dorsal vagal complex—part of the parasympathetic nervous system—engages, leading to shutdown and dissociation.

Dan Siegel, a leader in interpersonal neurobiology, highlights how dissociation disrupts integration across brain regions, fragmenting experience and sense of self. This fragmentation can make it challenging to stay grounded in the present or to access emotions safely.

Dissociation as a Barrier in Trauma Recovery

While dissociation protects in the moment, it can become a barrier when healing trauma. It prevents full processing of traumatic memories because those experiences remain split off from conscious awareness.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, emphasizes the importance of reconnecting with the body to heal dissociation. Trauma is stored in the body, and only through somatic awareness can these dissociated parts be integrated.

How to Come Back: Somatic Tools for Grounding and Integration

Coming back from dissociation means re-establishing safety in your body and nervous system. Here are practical, research-backed methods to start that process:

  • Tracking Sensations: Notice subtle physical sensations without judgment. This builds interoception, the sense of the body’s internal state, which Dan Siegel calls essential for integration.
  • Grounding Exercises: Techniques like feeling your feet on the floor, noticing textures, or controlled breathing help engage the ventral vagal state—a calm, connected nervous system state described by Stephen Porges.
  • Movement and Expression: Gentle movement, stretching, or shaking can discharge trauma energy held in the body, a core principle in Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing.
  • Safe Relational Connection: Healing is often relational. Deb Dana’s work shows how attuned relationships regulate the nervous system, making it easier to stay present.

Using Internal Family Systems (IFS) to Understand Dissociated Parts

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model offers a compassionate framework for working with dissociation. Instead of trying to eliminate dissociated parts, IFS invites you to acknowledge and dialogue with them. This acceptance reduces internal conflict and fosters integration.

In trauma, some parts may carry pain, fear, or protective roles. Recognizing these roles helps you meet these parts with curiosity rather than fear.

Avoiding Common Pitfalls: Why Traditional Talk Therapy May Not Be Enough

Many who have tried traditional therapy find themselves stuck because talk therapy alone often can’t reach dissociated or somatically stored trauma. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy is one exception that uses bilateral stimulation to access and process trauma safely.

However, without somatic regulation and nervous system work, trauma can remain lodged in the body, causing symptoms like anxiety, numbness, or flashbacks.

Final Thoughts: Reclaiming Presence and Wholeness

"Dissociation isn’t a failure—it’s a survival strategy. Healing demands we gently return to our bodies, learning to feel safe again, step by step." – The Oracle Lover

Recovering from dissociation takes patience and a willingness to meet your body where it is. Grounding in the present, building nervous system resilience, and integrating dissociated parts can restore a coherent sense of self.

As Gabor Maté reminds us, trauma recovery isn’t about erasing pain but about deeply understanding and compassionately working with the body’s wisdom. Somatic healing offers a path back to your life, your presence, and your aliveness.

For those feeling stuck after therapy, incorporating somatic approaches alongside psychological insight can unlock new doors to healing.

faqs.a: Dissociation is a protective response to overwhelming trauma where the mind disconnects from the body or reality to shield itself from distressing experiences.

faqs.q: How does dissociation affect the body?
faqs.a: Dissociation can cause numbness, out-of-body sensations, or memory gaps, reflecting the nervous system's shutdown or freeze response to threat.

faqs.q: Can somatic healing help with dissociation?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic healing focuses on reconnecting with bodily sensations and regulating the nervous system, which are essential for integrating dissociated trauma parts.

faqs.q: Why might talk therapy alone not resolve dissociation?
faqs.a: Because dissociation involves bodily and neurological disconnection, talk therapy without somatic or nervous system regulation often can’t access or integrate these split-off parts.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and mind, offering insights crucial for somatic healing.

metaTitle: Dissociation and Trauma: How to Heal and Ground Yourself
metaDescription: Learn what dissociation feels like in trauma and discover somatic tools to come back to your body and heal effectively.
author: The Oracle Lover
